stage.name: Decode: turn instruction bits into backend evidence
stage.description: A decode-stage lesson that explains compressed expansion, decoder output, scoreboard entries, CSR context, exceptions, and the handoff into issue.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

content.title: Decode: turn instruction bits into backend evidence
content.subtitle: The decode stage receives frontend instruction entries and turns them into information that issue and the scoreboard can use.
content.thesis: Decode is where an instruction stops being just bits and becomes a structured claim about operands, operation, exceptions, and backend work.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: Decode is the meaning boundary. Before decode, the frontend has delivered instruction data. After decode, the backend receives structured information. That sounds abstract, so make it concrete: compressed instructions may be expanded, the decoder builds a control structure, and the result becomes a scoreboard entry that later stages can track.

actions.text: Decode is the meaning boundary. Before decode, the frontend has delivered instruction data. After decode, the backend receives structured information. That sounds abstract, so make it concrete: compressed instructions may be expanded, the decoder builds a control structure, and the result becomes a scoreboard entry that later stages can track.

content.title: From frontend entry to issue-ready record
content.subtitle: ID_STAGE sits between instruction delivery and backend scheduling.

steps.title: Receive from fetch
steps.body: The frontend transmits instruction entries toward decode.

steps.title: Expand if compressed
steps.body: The compressed decoder checks and expands compressed RISC-V instructions.

steps.title: Decode operation
steps.body: The main decoder identifies operation class, operands, and control information.

steps.title: Build scoreboard entry
steps.body: Decoded information is packaged so issue and later stages can track it.

steps.title: Send to ISSUE_STAGE
steps.body: The backend receives structured work, not raw instruction bits.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

content.speakerNotes: The decode handoff is a transformation chain. Fetch gives decode an instruction entry. The compressed decoder checks whether it needs expansion. The decoder identifies what the instruction wants to do. Then decode builds a scoreboard entry. This is why backend lessons talk about scoreboard entries instead of just opcodes: decode has already translated raw bits into tracked work.

actions.text: The decode handoff is a transformation chain. Fetch gives decode an instruction entry. The compressed decoder checks whether it needs expansion. The decoder identifies what the instruction wants to do. Then decode builds a scoreboard entry. This is why backend lessons talk about scoreboard entries instead of just opcodes: decode has already translated raw bits into tracked work.

scenes.title: Decode sits between frontend and issue

content.title: Decode is not execution
content.subtitle: It prepares information for issue; it does not itself make the instruction architecturally visible.

callouts.title: Input side
callouts.body: Instruction entries arrive from the frontend path.

callouts.title: Context side
callouts.body: CSR and controller context can influence legality, privilege, exceptions, and flush behavior.

content.takeaway: Decode answers what this instruction means, not whether it can retire.

assets.alt: Official CVA6 architecture overview block diagram
assets.caption: Official CVA6 architecture overview diagram from the CV32A60X design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: architecture and modules
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The architecture block diagram groups FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT, CONTROLLER, and CSRFILE as the core reading map.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: The map position matters. Decode is between frontend and issue. It is not execution, and it is not retirement. It also does not live alone. CSR context, controller flush requests, and exception information can matter. So a good decode explanation says both what it receives and what context can change the decoded record.

actions.text: The map position matters. Decode is between frontend and issue. It is not execution, and it is not retirement. It also does not live alone. CSR context, controller flush requests, and exception information can matter. So a good decode explanation says both what it receives and what context can change the decoded record.

scenes.title: Compressed expansion and main decoding are separate ideas

content.title: Do not merge the two jobs
content.subtitle: Compressed support changes instruction representation before the main decode result is built.

columns.title: Compressed decoder
columns.body: Checks whether an incoming instruction is compressed and produces an uncompressed instruction representation.

columns.title: Main decoder
columns.body: Turns the instruction into control fields and a scoreboard entry the backend can reason about.

content.takeaway: Expansion handles format; decode handles architectural and backend meaning.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: Compressed expansion and main decoding are related, but not the same. The compressed decoder handles instruction format. It turns a compressed instruction into an uncompressed representation. The main decoder handles meaning. It identifies the operation and builds the control information that the backend will track. Mixing those two jobs makes later debug conversations fuzzy.

actions.text: Compressed expansion and main decoding are related, but not the same. The compressed decoder handles instruction format. It turns a compressed instruction into an uncompressed representation. The main decoder handles meaning. It identifies the operation and builds the control information that the backend will track. Mixing those two jobs makes later debug conversations fuzzy.

content.title: Legality and context can enter before execution
content.subtitle: Decode is allowed to carry exception information forward when the source supports it.

cards.title: Illegal instruction
cards.body: The decoder can detect illegal instruction cases and attach cause information.

cards.title: CSR context
cards.body: Privilege, trap, and extension status from CSR-related context can influence legality.

cards.title: Controller flush
cards.body: Control logic may request flush behavior around the decode boundary.

cards.title: Scoreboard payload
cards.body: Exception fields travel with the instruction record toward later precise handling.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: Decode is not only a happy-path translator. It can attach exception evidence, especially for illegal instruction style cases, while also considering context such as privilege and extension status. The key is that this evidence travels with the instruction record. Later, commit can use that record to keep architectural state precise.

actions.text: Decode is not only a happy-path translator. It can attach exception evidence, especially for illegal instruction style cases, while also considering context such as privilege and extension status. The key is that this evidence travels with the instruction record. Later, commit can use that record to keep architectural state precise.

content.title: Five questions before reading issue
content.subtitle: If these answers are unclear, backend behavior will look more mysterious than it really is.

items.title: What came from fetch?
items.body: Identify the instruction entry and any already-carried exception state.

items.title: Was it compressed?
items.body: Separate format expansion from semantic decode.

items.title: Which control fields are produced?
items.body: Know what issue and execute will depend on later.

items.title: Which context matters?
items.body: CSR and controller context can affect legality and flush handling.

items.title: What is sent onward?
items.body: The backend consumes a scoreboard-ready record, not just raw bits.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: Use this checklist before entering issue. What came from fetch? Was it compressed? Which control fields were produced? Which CSR or controller context matters? And what exactly is sent onward? Once those answers are clear, issue becomes a hazard and readiness problem, not a guessing game about opcodes.

actions.text: Use this checklist before entering issue. What came from fetch? Was it compressed? Which control fields were produced? Which CSR or controller context matters? And what exactly is sent onward? Once those answers are clear, issue becomes a hazard and readiness problem, not a guessing game about opcodes.

questions.question: What is the best short description of ID_STAGE?

options.label: It decodes frontend instructions and sends structured information to ISSUE_STAGE

questions.analysis: ID_STAGE transforms fetched instructions into backend-consumable decoded information.

questions.question: Why separate compressed decoding from main decoding?

options.label: One handles instruction format expansion, while the other builds semantic/backend control information
They are two names for commit
Main decoding only reads cache lines

questions.analysis: Compressed expansion and semantic decode are distinct responsibilities.

questions.question: Which information can be relevant around decode?

options.label: Final architectural retirement of all stores

questions.analysis: Decode can use context and attach exception evidence, but final retirement is commit work.

questions.question: What is the professional way to describe decode output?

options.label: A scoreboard-ready record containing decoded control and tracking information
A random opcode string sent directly to memory

questions.analysis: The backend tracks decoded instruction work through structured entries.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: The decode quiz checks whether you can keep meaning, format, context, and retirement separate. Decode creates the record; issue decides when that record can move; execute produces results; commit retires state. That separation is the backbone of the next three lessons.

actions.text: The decode quiz checks whether you can keep meaning, format, context, and retirement separate. Decode creates the record; issue decides when that record can move; execute produces results; commit retires state. That separation is the backbone of the next three lessons.

content.title: You can now explain what issue receives
content.subtitle: Issue does not receive mystery bits. It receives decoded, trackable instruction records.
takeaways: Decode transforms frontend entries into backend-ready control information.
Compressed expansion and semantic decoding are separate responsibilities.
Exception and context information can be carried forward in the instruction record.
content.nextStep: Next: Issue to Execute, where hazards, operands, and functional units shape backend progress.
content.closing: Decode is where instruction bits become accountable backend work.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: Decode prepares the backend story. It expands, decodes, attaches context, and sends a trackable entry forward. Next, we finally reach issue and execute. The question will shift again: now that the backend knows what the instruction is, when is it safe to let it run?

actions.text: Decode prepares the backend story. It expands, decodes, attaches context, and sends a trackable entry forward. Next, we finally reach issue and execute. The question will shift again: now that the backend knows what the instruction is, when is it safe to let it run?
